question: A histology slide shows a lobulated thoracic organ with a basophilic outer zone and a pale inner zone. Which structure is most consistent with this description?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Thymic cortex densely packed with immature T cells
options.rationale: Correct because the thymus has a basophilic cortex with densely packed immature thymocytes and a paler medulla; this matches the lecture description of thymic zones.

options.text: Lymph node cortex containing follicles with germinal centers
options.rationale: Wrong because lymph nodes have follicles in the cortex but the lecture specifies a lobulated thoracic organ with cortex and medulla for the thymus; changing location to thorax and epithelial nurse cells would make this correct.

options.text: Spleen white pulp surrounding central arterioles with PALS
options.rationale: Wrong because the spleen's white pulp organization differs from a lobulated thoracic organ; if the specimen were in the left upper quadrant and showed red pulp, this option would be plausible.

options.text: Palatine tonsil with crypts and unencapsulated lymphoid tissue
options.rationale: Wrong because tonsils are unencapsulated MALT with crypts rather than a bilobed lobulated thoracic organ; if the slide showed crypt epithelium, this would be correct.

options.text: Bone marrow with hematopoietic islands and adipose spaces
options.rationale: Wrong because bone marrow lacks the distinct cortex/medulla lobulation described; if the slide showed trabeculae and marrow sinusoids, this option would fit.

question: Which region of a lymph node primarily contains T lymphocytes and is the main site for antigen presentation to T cells?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Paracortex composed mainly of T lymphocytes and HEVs
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture identifies the paracortex as T cell–rich and the principal site for antigen presentation and T cell activation.

options.text: Cortical follicles primarily containing resting B lymphocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because follicles are B cell zones; if follicles lacked germinal centers and were described as paracortical, this would be correct.

options.text: Medullary cords containing plasma cells and antibody secreting cells
options.rationale: Wrong because medullary cords are plasma cell–rich and not the main T cell activation site; if the question asked about antibody secretion, this would be correct.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus where macrophages filter afferent lymph
options.rationale: Wrong because the subcapsular sinus contains macrophages and antigen transport but is not the primary T cell zone; if the stem asked about antigen capture from lymph, this would fit.

options.text: Hilum where efferent lymphatics and blood vessels exit node
options.rationale: Wrong because the hilum is an exit site for efferent lymphatics and vessels rather than a T cell activation zone; if the question asked about lymph outflow, this would be correct.

question: A student examines a thymus from an adult and notes increased adipose infiltration and reduced lymphoid tissue. Which process described in the lecture best explains this finding?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Age related involution with fatty replacement of thymus
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states the thymus involutes with age, showing adipose infiltration and depletion of lymphoid tissue.

options.text: Reactive hyperplasia with expansion of cortical thymocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because reactive hyperplasia would increase lymphoid cellularity rather than adipose; if the specimen showed enlarged cortex and germinal centers, this would be correct.

options.text: Autoimmune destruction causing medullary epithelial loss
options.rationale: Wrong because autoimmune destruction would not primarily cause adipose replacement; if there were specific autoimmune markers and epithelial cell loss, this could be plausible.

options.text: Chronic infection producing persistent thymic enlargement
options.rationale: Wrong because chronic infection typically enlarges lymphoid tissue rather than causing fatty involution; if the history included chronic systemic infection, enlargement would be expected.

options.text: Congenital hypoplasia with absent thymic lobulation
options.rationale: Wrong because congenital hypoplasia would present early in life and not as age-related adipose infiltration; if the patient were an infant with immunodeficiency, this would be correct.

question: Which structure in the spleen surrounds central arterioles and is predominantly T cell rich?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Periarteriolar lymphoid sheath surrounding central arterioles
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture names the PALS as the T cell–rich sheath around central arterioles in the spleen.

options.text: Marginal zone containing macrophages and antigen presenting cells
options.rationale: Wrong because the marginal zone is between red and white pulp and contains macrophages; if the question asked about antigen capture from blood, this would be correct.

options.text: Red pulp composed mainly of splenic cords and sinusoids
options.rationale: Wrong because red pulp filters blood and removes aged RBCs rather than being T cell–rich; if the stem asked about erythrocyte clearance, this would be correct.

options.text: Germinal center within white pulp primarily of B cells
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers are B cell–rich follicles; if the question asked about B cell clonal expansion, this would be correct.

options.text: Trabecular artery supplying penicillar arterioles and sinuses
options.rationale: Wrong because trabecular arteries are vascular structures not T cell zones; if the question asked about splenic blood supply, this would be correct.

question: A lymphocyte recirculation diagram shows mature B cells leaving primary lymphoid tissue to enter peripheral organs. Which primary organ is the site of B cell maturation according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Bone marrow as site of B lymphocyte maturation and production
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states bone marrow produces blood cells and is the site of B lymphocyte maturation.

options.text: Thymus as primary site for B cell development and selection
options.rationale: Wrong because the thymus is for T cell maturation; if the lecture indicated B cell receptor rearrangement occurred there, this would be correct.

options.text: Spleen white pulp where naive B cells first develop
options.rationale: Wrong because white pulp supports adaptive responses but is not the primary site of B cell maturation; if the stem described antigen-driven maturation, this would fit.

options.text: MALT Peyer patches as the origin of naive B lymphocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because MALT contains mature lymphocytes and responses at mucosa but not initial B cell maturation; if the question asked about mucosal activation, this would be correct.

options.text: Lymph node paracortex where B cells undergo initial maturation
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortex is T cell–rich; if the stem described germinal center formation in follicles, that would indicate B cell activation rather than maturation.

question: A patient has a biopsy of a palatine tonsil showing unencapsulated lymphoid aggregates with crypts. Which category of lymphoid tissue does this represent?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Mucosa associated lymphoid tissue including palatine tonsils
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture defines MALT as unencapsulated lymphoid aggregates at mucosal surfaces, including palatine tonsils.

options.text: Primary lymphoid organ similar to thymus with cortex medulla
options.rationale: Wrong because primary lymphoid organs are generative (bone marrow, thymus); if the tissue were encapsulated and lobulated, this would be correct.

options.text: Spleen white pulp with PALS and germinal center follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because spleen white pulp is encapsulated and vascularly organized; if the biopsy were from the spleen hilum, this would be correct.

options.text: Lymph node cortex with subcapsular sinus and follicles present
options.rationale: Wrong because lymph nodes are encapsulated and have afferent/efferent lymphatics; if the specimen had a capsule and trabeculae, this would be correct.

options.text: Diffuse lymphatic tissue of bone marrow with hematopoiesis ongoing
options.rationale: Wrong because bone marrow is hematopoietic and not characterized by crypts; if the sample showed marrow sinusoids and erythroid islands, this would fit.

question: Which vascular structure in lymph nodes allows naive lymphocytes from blood to enter lymphoid tissue directly?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: High endothelial venules permit lymphocyte entry from blood
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture highlights HEVs as specialized venules that allow lymphocyte trafficking from blood into lymph node paracortex.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus where afferent lymph first arrives
options.rationale: Wrong because the subcapsular sinus receives lymph from afferent vessels rather than blood-derived lymphocytes; if the question asked about lymph arrival, this would be correct.

options.text: Efferent lymphatic vessel that drains lymph out of node
options.rationale: Wrong because efferent vessels carry lymph away and do not mediate entry from blood; if the stem asked about lymph exit, this would be correct.

options.text: Trabecular sinus conducting lymph through nodal trabeculae
options.rationale: Wrong because trabecular sinuses are conduits for lymph flow, not blood-to-lymphocyte entry; if the question asked about lymph flow pathways, this would fit.

options.text: Medullary sinus containing macrophages filtering lymph
options.rationale: Wrong because medullary sinuses filter lymph and contain macrophages, not the specialized endothelium for blood entry; if the stem asked about antigen filtration, this would be correct.

question: A patient with splenic dysfunction has impaired clearance of aged erythrocytes. Which splenic compartment is primarily responsible for filtering blood and removing damaged red cells?
cited_learning_objective: Recite the flow of blood through the spleen.

options.text: Red pulp composed of splenic cords and sinusoids filters blood
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states red pulp filters blood to remove particulate matter and aged erythrocytes.

options.text: White pulp where adaptive responses to blood antigens occur
options.rationale: Wrong because white pulp mounts adaptive immune responses rather than filtering erythrocytes; if the question asked about antigen response to bloodborne pathogens, this would be correct.

options.text: PALS surrounding central arterioles primarily mediates T cell responses
options.rationale: Wrong because PALS is T cell–rich and not the main erythrocyte filter; if the stem asked about T cell localization, this would be correct.

options.text: Marginal zone capturing antigens between red and white pulp
options.rationale: Wrong because marginal zone captures antigens and contains macrophages but is not the primary site of erythrocyte clearance; if the question focused on antigen capture, this would fit.

options.text: Sheathed capillaries directly returning blood to splenic vein
options.rationale: Wrong because sheathed capillaries are involved in blood flow and macrophage sheaths but red pulp is the main filtering compartment; if the stem described vascular return, this would be correct.

question: During an immune response, which lymph node structure expands with proliferating B cells forming germinal centers?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Cortical follicles develop germinal centers with proliferating B cells
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates follicles in the cortex form germinal centers during B cell activation and clonal expansion.

options.text: Paracortex enlarges due to plasma cell accumulation and secretion
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortex is T cell–rich; if plasma cells accumulated in medullary cords, that would be correct.

options.text: Medullary cords expand with naive B lymphocytes and macrophages
options.rationale: Wrong because medullary cords contain plasma cells and precursors rather than naive B cells; if the stem described antibody secretion, this would be correct.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus forms germinal centers under antigen exposure
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers form in follicles not in subcapsular sinuses; if follicles were absent and ectopic GC formed in sinuses, this would be plausible.

options.text: Hilum develops follicles that produce memory B lymphocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because the hilum is vascular and efferent exit site, not a follicle location; if follicles were mislocalized to the hilum, this would be correct.

question: A flow diagram shows lymph entering via afferent vessels, passing through subcapsular sinus, cortical sinuses, and exiting via efferent vessels. Which sequence best describes lymph flow through a lymph node?
cited_learning_objective: Recite the flow of lymph through a lymph node.

options.text: Afferent lymphatics to subcapsular sinus to efferent lymphatics
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture outlines lymph entering via afferent lymphatics into the subcapsular sinus, traversing cortical and medullary sinuses, and exiting via efferent lymphatics.

options.text: Efferent lymphatics to subcapsular sinus to afferent lymphatics
options.rationale: Wrong because efferent vessels are exit pathways; reversing the flow would be incorrect unless the node anatomy were surgically altered.

options.text: High endothelial venules to medullary cords to efferent lymphatics
options.rationale: Wrong because HEVs mediate blood entry of lymphocytes, not primary lymph flow; if the question asked about lymphocyte trafficking from blood, HEVs would be relevant.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus directly to medullary cords then to artery
options.rationale: Wrong because lymph does not drain into arteries; if the stem described vascular-lymphatic fistula, this would be correct.

options.text: Trabecular sinus to afferent lymphatics to subcapsular sinus
options.rationale: Wrong because trabecular sinuses are internal conduits and afferent vessels bring lymph into the node; swapping these would contradict normal flow.

question: Which cell type in the thymic medulla is uniquely organized into Hassall corpuscles and contributes to regulatory T cell development?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Thymic epithelial cells forming Hassall corpuscles in medulla
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes Hassall's corpuscles as groups of epithelial cells in the thymic medulla important for regulatory T cell development.

options.text: Cortical macrophages clearing apoptotic immature thymocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because macrophages are abundant in cortex and clear dying thymocytes; if the question asked about apoptotic cell clearance, macrophages would be correct.

options.text: Dendritic cells in cortex presenting self antigens to thymocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because dendritic cells present antigen but Hassall corpuscles are epithelial; if the stem focused on negative selection, dendritic cells would be relevant.

options.text: Plasma cells secreting antibodies within thymic medullary cords
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells are not a thymic medullary feature; if the tissue were a lymph node medulla, plasma cells would be expected.

options.text: Endothelial cells forming high endothelial venules in medulla
options.rationale: Wrong because HEVs are characteristic of lymph nodes, not thymic medulla; if the organ were a lymph node, HEVs would be correct.

question: A patient with edema has increased interstitial fluid formation. According to the lecture, which vascular change would most directly increase lymph formation?
cited_learning_objective: Formation of Lymph

options.text: Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure promoting fluid filtration
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explains lymph forms from interstitial fluid when more fluid filters from capillaries into tissues; increased hydrostatic pressure raises filtration and lymph formation.

options.text: Decreased capillary permeability reducing protein leakage into interstitium
options.rationale: Wrong because decreased permeability would lower interstitial protein and fluid accumulation; if permeability increased instead, lymph formation would rise.

options.text: Elevated plasma oncotic pressure drawing fluid into capillaries
options.rationale: Wrong because higher plasma oncotic pressure pulls fluid back into capillaries, reducing interstitial fluid; if oncotic pressure fell, lymph formation would increase.

options.text: Constriction of lymphatic vessels preventing lymph drainage
options.rationale: Wrong because impaired lymph drainage causes accumulation but does not increase formation; if lymphatic obstruction were present, edema would worsen but formation rate might be unchanged.

options.text: Increased venous pressure decreasing capillary hydrostatic pressure
options.rationale: Wrong because increased venous pressure typically raises capillary hydrostatic pressure rather than decreasing it; if venous pressure decreased, filtration would fall.

question: A patient with splenectomy is at risk for certain infections. Based on the lecture, which splenic function's loss most directly increases susceptibility to bloodborne encapsulated organisms?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Loss of splenic white pulp adaptive responses to blood antigens
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states white pulp mounts adaptive immune responses against bloodborne agents; losing this reduces clearance of encapsulated organisms.

options.text: Loss of red pulp erythrocyte recycling and iron return to marrow
options.rationale: Wrong because red pulp filters RBCs and recycles iron but this function is less directly tied to susceptibility to encapsulated bacteria; if the question asked about anemia risk, this would be correct.

options.text: Loss of PALS causing impaired thymic T cell maturation
options.rationale: Wrong because PALS is splenic T cell zone and does not mediate thymic maturation; if thymic function were impaired, T cell development would be affected.

options.text: Loss of sheathed capillaries increasing systemic blood pressure
options.rationale: Wrong because sheathed capillaries relate to splenic microcirculation not systemic pressure; if vascular resistance changed significantly, blood pressure might be affected.

options.text: Loss of marginal zone reducing antigen capture from lymphatics
options.rationale: Wrong because the marginal zone captures antigens from blood rather than lymphatics; if marginal zone macrophages were intact, antigen capture would be preserved.

question: A biopsy of a lymph node shows abundant plasma cells in medullary cords and few B cells in cortex. Which functional role described in the lecture best explains the medullary plasma cell location?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Medullary cords house plasma cells secreting antibodies into sinuses
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes medullary cords contain plasma cells and their precursors which secrete antibody into medullary sinuses.

options.text: Paracortex contains plasma cells that migrate to follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortex is T cell–rich; if plasma cells were described migrating from paracortex to medulla, this would be plausible.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus is primary site of plasma cell differentiation
options.rationale: Wrong because subcapsular sinus filters lymph and is not the main plasma cell differentiation site; if germinal centers were absent and differentiation occurred ectopically, this could be correct.

options.text: Cortical follicles are where plasma cells reside permanently
options.rationale: Wrong because follicles are B cell zones and germinal centers produce plasmablasts that migrate to medulla; if the stem described follicular plasmacytosis, this would fit.

options.text: Hilum contains plasma cells that secrete antibodies into blood
options.rationale: Wrong because the hilum is vascular exit site and not a plasma cell niche; if plasma cells were mislocalized to hilum, this would be unusual.

question: A researcher traces lymph drainage from peripheral tissues to the circulation. Which duct drains most of the body and empties into the left subclavian vein as described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Thoracic duct draining into the left subclavian vein near jugular
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states the thoracic (left) duct drains most lymph and empties into the left subclavian vein.

options.text: Right lymphatic duct draining all lymph into right subclavian vein
options.rationale: Wrong because the right lymphatic duct drains only the right upper quadrant; if the question specified right-sided drainage only, this would be correct.

options.text: Cisterna chyli draining directly into right internal jugular vein
options.rationale: Wrong because the cisterna chyli is a dilated sac feeding the thoracic duct, not draining into the jugular; if the anatomy were variant, this might be plausible.

options.text: Efferent lymphatic trunks emptying into hepatic portal vein
options.rationale: Wrong because efferent lymphatics drain nodes and ultimately ducts, not directly into portal circulation; if the stem described gut lymphatics, chyle enters thoracic duct.

options.text: Afferent lymphatic vessels that directly join subclavian veins
options.rationale: Wrong because afferent lymphatics enter lymph nodes and do not directly join veins; if surgical anastomosis existed, direct venous entry could occur.

question: A patient presents with recurrent mucosal infections of the gut. Which organized lymphoid structure in the small intestine is most likely involved in mucosal immune responses?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Peyer patches as gut associated lymphoid tissue in ileum
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture identifies Peyer patches as GALT in the small intestine involved in mucosal immune responses.

options.text: Palatine tonsils located in oropharynx protecting upper airway
options.rationale: Wrong because palatine tonsils are oropharyngeal MALT, not small intestinal; if infections were oropharyngeal, tonsils would be implicated.

options.text: Bronchus associated lymphoid tissue in lower respiratory mucosa
options.rationale: Wrong because BALT is respiratory MALT; if pulmonary infections predominated, BALT would be relevant.

options.text: Mesenteric lymph nodes draining intestinal lymph and antigens
options.rationale: Wrong because mesenteric nodes drain gut lymph but Peyer patches are organized GALT; if the question asked about lymph drainage, mesenteric nodes would be correct.

options.text: Appendiceal lymphoid aggregates functioning as isolated MALT
options.rationale: Wrong because the appendix contains lymphoid aggregates but Peyer patches are the classic ileal GALT; if the stem specified appendiceal pathology, this would fit.

question: A lab slide shows immunohistochemical staining: CD20 positive follicles and CD3 positive paracortex. Which statement best matches the cellular distribution?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: B cells localize to follicles while T cells occupy paracortex
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture demonstrates CD20 marking B cell follicles in cortex and CD3 marking T cells in the paracortex.

options.text: T cells form germinal centers while B cells occupy medulla
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers are B cell structures; if germinal centers stained for CD3, that would indicate atypical T cell proliferation.

options.text: Plasma cells predominate in cortical follicles producing antibodies
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells are mainly in medullary cords; if follicles matured into plasmacytic foci, plasma cells could be present.

options.text: Macrophages exclusively occupy paracortex clearing apoptotic cells
options.rationale: Wrong because macrophages are present in sinuses and cortex as well; if the stem described abundant apoptotic thymocytes, macrophage presence would increase.

options.text: Endothelial cells in follicles form high endothelial venules
options.rationale: Wrong because HEVs are in paracortex not follicles; if the question targeted lymphocyte entry from blood, HEVs would be relevant.

question: A patient has a thymic tumor that disrupts cortical epithelial cell function. Which thymic process described in the lecture would be most directly impaired?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Positive selection and maturation of immature thymocytes in cortex
options.rationale: Correct because cortical epithelial cells interact with developing thymocytes to ensure maturation and positive selection; disruption impairs T cell maturation.

options.text: Peripheral B cell class switching within germinal centers
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal center B cell processes occur in lymph nodes and spleen, not thymic cortex; if the tumor invaded lymphoid follicles, B cell responses could be affected.

options.text: Antibody secretion by plasma cells in medullary cords
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cell antibody secretion is a medullary lymph node function; thymic cortical epithelial disruption would not directly impair plasma cell function.

options.text: Marginal zone macrophage antigen capture in spleen
options.rationale: Wrong because marginal zone functions are splenic; if the tumor metastasized to spleen, marginal zone could be affected.

options.text: Lymph formation from interstitial fluid in peripheral tissues
options.rationale: Wrong because lymph formation is a vascular/tissue fluid process independent of thymic epithelial cells; only systemic vascular changes would alter lymph formation.

question: A patient with a blocked thoracic duct develops chylous ascites. Based on the lecture, where does the thoracic duct normally collect lymph before draining into the venous circulation?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Cisterna chyli collects lymph from lower body before thoracic duct
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes the cisterna chyli as the dilated sac that drains lymph from lower body into the thoracic duct.

options.text: Right lymphatic duct collecting lymph from entire body
options.rationale: Wrong because the right lymphatic duct drains only the right upper quadrant; if anatomy were variant, a right-sided duct could be larger.

options.text: Efferent lymphatic trunks draining directly into hepatic portal vein
options.rationale: Wrong because efferent trunks drain into collecting ducts not portal vein; if surgical shunt existed, direct portal drainage could occur.

options.text: Subclavian lymphatic plexus adjacent to axillary nodes
options.rationale: Wrong because subclavian veins receive duct drainage but there is no named subclavian lymphatic plexus as primary collector; if the stem described axillary drainage, this would be relevant.

options.text: Afferent lymphatic capillaries within intestinal villi directly to vein
options.rationale: Wrong because intestinal lacteals drain into lymphatics then cisterna chyli, not directly into veins; if the question described portal venous absorption, this would be different.

question: A patient has a lymph node biopsy showing absent paracortex and reduced HEVs. Which immune process described in the lecture would be most affected?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Entry of naive lymphocytes from blood into lymph node paracortex
options.rationale: Correct because HEVs in the paracortex mediate lymphocyte entry from blood; loss impairs naive lymphocyte trafficking and antigen surveillance.

options.text: Antibody secretion by plasma cells in medullary cords
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cell antibody secretion occurs in medullary cords and would be secondarily affected only if B cell activation failed; if medulla were destroyed, secretion would drop.

options.text: Germinal center formation in cortical follicles producing B cells
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers are in follicles and could still form if paracortex absent; if T cell help were absent due to paracortical loss, germinal centers might be impaired.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus macrophage antigen capture from afferent lymph
options.rationale: Wrong because subcapsular sinus macrophages capture antigen independently of HEVs; if afferent lymph flow ceased, antigen capture would be reduced.

options.text: Efferent lymphatic drainage of lymph node to thoracic duct
options.rationale: Wrong because efferent drainage is a structural outflow function not directly dependent on HEVs; if efferent vessels were ligated, drainage would be impaired.

question: A clinician examines a patient with recurrent infections and finds markedly reduced circulating T cells due to thymic failure. Which lecture-described thymic function explains this immunodeficiency?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Thymus educates immature T cells and enforces self tolerance
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explains the thymus matures thymocytes and deletes self-reactive clones, so failure reduces mature T cell output and causes susceptibility to infection.

options.text: Bone marrow fails to produce B lymphocytes causing humoral defect
options.rationale: Wrong because the stem specifies reduced T cells from thymic failure; if bone marrow output of B cells were low, humoral immunity would be affected instead.

options.text: Spleen inability to filter blood causing increased bacteremia
options.rationale: Wrong because splenic dysfunction increases susceptibility to encapsulated organisms but does not primarily reduce circulating T cells; if spleen were removed, bacteremia risk rises.

options.text: Lymph node paracortex hyperplasia consuming circulating T cells
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortical hyperplasia would not deplete circulating T cells; if nodes sequestered T cells, counts might fall but thymic failure is more direct.

options.text: MALT depletion leading to loss of mucosal IgA production
options.rationale: Wrong because MALT affects mucosal immunity and IgA, not systemic T cell maturation; if mucosal infections predominated, MALT dysfunction would be implicated.

question: A pathology slide shows macrophage-rich medullary sinuses in a lymph node. Which lecture-described role best fits macrophages in medullary sinuses?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Macrophages in medullary sinuses filter lymph and phagocytose debris
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes medullary sinuses contain macrophages that filter lymph and remove particulate matter.

options.text: Macrophages in cortex primarily produce antibodies as plasma cells
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells, not macrophages, secrete antibodies; if macrophages transdifferentiated into plasma cells, this would be different.

options.text: Macrophages in paracortex present antigen to naive B cells
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortex is T cell–rich and antigen presentation there targets T cells; if the stem described dendritic cells, antigen presentation to T cells would be correct.

options.text: Macrophages in follicles drive germinal center B cell proliferation
options.rationale: Wrong because follicular dendritic cells support germinal centers, not macrophages; if follicular dendritic cells were present, B cell proliferation would be supported.

options.text: Macrophages in hilum regulate efferent lymphocyte egress
options.rationale: Wrong because hilum is vascular exit site and macrophages there are not the primary regulators of lymphocyte egress; if egress signals changed, lymphocyte trafficking could be altered.

question: A patient with chronic inflammation has enlarged lymph nodes with prominent germinal centers. Which lecture-described process is most responsible for germinal center formation?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Antigen driven B cell activation and clonal expansion in follicles
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explains germinal centers form from stimulated B cells undergoing clonal expansion in follicles.

options.text: T cell independent plasma cell differentiation in medullary cords
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers are T cell–dependent B cell structures; if an antigen were T cell independent, extrafollicular plasmablast responses might occur instead.

options.text: Macrophage proliferation in subcapsular sinus causing nodal enlargement
options.rationale: Wrong because macrophage proliferation alone does not create germinal centers; if macrophages presented antigen to B cells in follicles, they could contribute indirectly.

options.text: Increased lymph flow through efferent vessels producing follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because lymph flow changes do not directly induce germinal centers; antigen exposure and B cell activation are required.

options.text: Thymic cortical hyperplasia spilling immature T cells into node
options.rationale: Wrong because thymic hyperplasia affects T cell development and not germinal center B cell formation; if T cell help were absent, germinal centers would be impaired.

question: A surgical specimen shows sheathed capillaries surrounded by macrophages in the spleen. According to the lecture, what is the significance of these sheathed capillaries?
cited_learning_objective: Recite the flow of blood through the spleen.

options.text: Sheathed capillaries facilitate blood filtration and macrophage contact
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes sheathed capillaries surrounded by macrophages that contribute to splenic blood filtration and removal of particulate matter.

options.text: Sheathed capillaries are primary sites of lymphocyte recirculation into blood
options.rationale: Wrong because lymphocyte recirculation occurs via HEVs in lymph nodes, not sheathed capillaries; if the stem described lymphocyte egress from spleen, other structures would be implicated.

options.text: Sheathed capillaries produce plasma cells that migrate to medulla
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells are not produced by capillaries; if the spleen had ectopic plasmacytosis, plasma cells could be found nearby.

options.text: Sheathed capillaries drain directly into thoracic duct lymphatics
options.rationale: Wrong because splenic capillaries are part of blood circulation and do not drain into lymph ducts; if vascular-lymphatic connections existed, this would be unusual.

options.text: Sheathed capillaries form germinal centers within white pulp follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers are B cell follicle structures, not capillary sheaths; if follicles were vascularized differently, capillaries might be adjacent.

question: A patient with impaired lymphatic valves develops peripheral edema. Which lymphatic structural feature described in the lecture normally prevents backflow of lymph?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Valves within lymphatic vessels prevent lymph backflow and reflux
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes lymphatic vessels have valves that prevent backflow and help unidirectional lymph transport.

options.text: Anchoring fibers connecting capillaries to surrounding tissue prevent reflux
options.rationale: Wrong because anchoring fibers keep lymphatic endothelial flaps open but valves are the primary anti-backflow structures; if anchoring fibers were absent, capillary collapse could occur.

options.text: High endothelial venules in lymph nodes prevent lymphatic backflow
options.rationale: Wrong because HEVs mediate lymphocyte entry from blood, not lymph flow directionality; if HEVs were dysfunctional, lymphocyte trafficking would be affected.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus valves within lymph nodes regulate lymph direction
options.rationale: Wrong because subcapsular sinuses are spaces for lymph flow and do not contain valves; if nodal sinuses had valve-like structures, flow might be locally regulated.

options.text: Thoracic duct sphincter at venous junction prevents retrograde flow
options.rationale: Wrong because the thoracic duct drains into veins but valves in lymphatics, not a sphincter, primarily prevent retrograde flow; if a venous pressure gradient reversed, retrograde flow could occur.

question: A patient with a congenital absence of thymic epithelial cells would most likely fail which central tolerance process described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Negative selection deleting self reactive T cell clones in medulla
options.rationale: Correct because thymic epithelial cells contribute to presentation of self-antigens and development of self-tolerance; their absence impairs deletion of self-reactive T cells.

options.text: Somatic hypermutation of B cell receptors in germinal centers
options.rationale: Wrong because somatic hypermutation occurs in germinal centers of lymph nodes and spleen, not thymus; if B cell follicles were absent, humoral affinity maturation would be affected.

options.text: Positive selection of B cells in bone marrow for antigen binding
options.rationale: Wrong because positive selection of B cells occurs in bone marrow and is independent of thymic epithelial cells; if bone marrow stromal cells were absent, B cell selection would be impaired.

options.text: Plasma cell migration to medullary cords for antibody secretion
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cell migration is a peripheral lymph node event and not central tolerance; if medullary cords were absent, antibody secretion would be reduced.

options.text: Marginal zone B cell capture of bloodborne antigens in spleen
options.rationale: Wrong because marginal zone functions are splenic and unrelated to thymic epithelial cell–mediated tolerance; if marginal zone macrophages were deficient, antigen capture would be impaired.

question: A clinician asks which organ is the primary site for T cell maturation and selection. Which concise answer aligns with the lecture learning objectives?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Thymus as the school for educating immature T lymphocytes
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explicitly describes the thymus as the organ where immature T cells mature, acquire receptors, and undergo selection.

options.text: Bone marrow as the exclusive site of T cell maturation and selection
options.rationale: Wrong because bone marrow produces lymphoid precursors but T cell maturation occurs in the thymus; if the stem indicated early lymphoid commitment only, bone marrow would be relevant.

options.text: Lymph node paracortex as the primary T cell maturation organ
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortex is a peripheral activation site for mature T cells, not the site of maturation; if the question asked about antigen-driven activation, paracortex would be correct.

options.text: Spleen PALS as the central organ for T cell education
options.rationale: Wrong because PALS is a T cell zone in spleen for responses to bloodborne antigens, not for thymic education; if the stem asked about splenic T cell localization, PALS would be relevant.

options.text: MALT Peyer patches as the organ where T cells are generated
options.rationale: Wrong because MALT contains mature lymphocytes responding at mucosa but does not generate naive T cells; if mucosal T cell activation were the focus, Peyer patches would be implicated.

question: A patient with impaired HEV function has reduced lymphocyte trafficking into lymph nodes. Which downstream lymph node process described in the lecture would be most directly reduced?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Naive lymphocyte entry and antigen surveillance within paracortex
options.rationale: Correct because HEVs mediate naive lymphocyte entry into paracortex for antigen surveillance; impaired HEVs reduce lymphocyte trafficking and immune surveillance.

options.text: Antibody secretion by medullary plasma cells into lymph sinuses
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cell antibody secretion depends on prior B cell activation and migration; if HEV impairment reduced T cell help, antibody production could be secondarily affected.

options.text: Germinal center somatic hypermutation independent of T cell help
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal center reactions are T cell–dependent; if HEV dysfunction eliminated T cell entry, germinal centers would be impaired but not independent.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus macrophage phagocytosis of circulating erythrocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because subcapsular macrophages filter lymph, not circulating erythrocytes; if erythrocyte clearance were needed, spleen red pulp would be primary.

options.text: Efferent lymphatic pumping increasing lymph return to circulation
options.rationale: Wrong because efferent lymphatic pumping is mechanical and not directly controlled by HEVs; if lymph flow decreased, efferent output would fall but HEVs are unrelated.

question: A patient with splenic marginal zone dysfunction shows impaired capture of blood antigens. Which lecture-described cell population in the marginal zone is primarily responsible for antigen capture?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Marginal zone macrophages and antigen presenting cells capture blood antigens
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates the marginal zone contains macrophages that capture antigens from the blood between red and white pulp.

options.text: PALS T cells capturing antigens directly from bloodstream
options.rationale: Wrong because PALS is T cell–rich and not the primary antigen capture site; if T cells directly sampled blood antigens, PALS would be implicated.

options.text: Red pulp cords filtering antigens via plasma cell secretion
options.rationale: Wrong because red pulp filters erythrocytes and debris, not primarily via plasma cell secretion; if plasma cells secreted opsonins locally, antigen handling could be affected.

options.text: Germinal center follicular dendritic cells capturing lymphatic antigens
options.rationale: Wrong because follicular dendritic cells capture antigens in follicles, not marginal zone blood antigens; if antigen moved into follicles, FDCs would retain it.

options.text: Sheathed capillary endothelial cells presenting antigens to B cells
options.rationale: Wrong because sheathed capillaries are surrounded by macrophages rather than endothelial antigen presentation; if endothelial cells expressed unusual antigen presentation molecules, this could change.

question: A patient with chronic lymphedema has dilated lymphatic capillaries and impaired anchoring fibers. According to the lecture, what is the role of anchoring fibers in lymphatic capillaries?
cited_learning_objective: Formation of Lymph

options.text: Anchoring fibers keep lymphatic endothelial flaps open during edema
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture describes anchoring fibers that attach lymphatic endothelial cells to surrounding tissue, helping flaps open to admit interstitial fluid.

options.text: Anchoring fibers form valves preventing lymph backflow in vessels
options.rationale: Wrong because valves within lymphatic vessels prevent backflow; if anchoring fibers were absent and valves failed, backflow could increase.

options.text: Anchoring fibers generate lymph propulsion via smooth muscle contraction
options.rationale: Wrong because lymph propulsion is aided by vessel contraction and external forces, not anchoring fibers; if smooth muscle were hyperactive, propulsion would change.

options.text: Anchoring fibers present antigen to dendritic cells in tissues
options.rationale: Wrong because anchoring fibers are structural, not antigen-presenting; if extracellular matrix proteins bound antigen, they might indirectly influence presentation.

options.text: Anchoring fibers connect lymphatics directly to venous endothelium
options.rationale: Wrong because anchoring fibers attach lymphatic endothelium to surrounding connective tissue, not veins; if surgical anastomosis existed, direct connections could form.

question: A patient with a localized mucosal infection in the bronchus is most likely to activate which organized lymphoid tissue described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: Bronchus associated lymphoid tissue as part of MALT in airways
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture lists BALT as bronchus-associated lymphoid tissue within MALT that protects mucosal surfaces like the respiratory tract.

options.text: Peyer patches in ileum responding to bronchial pathogens
options.rationale: Wrong because Peyer patches are intestinal GALT; if the infection were enteric, Peyer patches would be relevant.

options.text: Palatine tonsils draining lower airway secretions into follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because palatine tonsils are oropharyngeal MALT and not primary bronchial lymphoid tissue; if infection involved oropharynx, tonsils would be implicated.

options.text: Spleen white pulp mounting mucosal immune response in lung
options.rationale: Wrong because spleen responds to bloodborne antigens, not localized bronchial mucosal infections; if pathogens entered bloodstream, spleen would respond.

options.text: Mesenteric lymph nodes as primary responders to bronchial antigens
options.rationale: Wrong because mesenteric nodes drain gut lymph, not bronchial mucosa; if antigens reached gut-associated lymphatics, mesenteric nodes would be involved.

question: A patient with a lymphatic obstruction proximal to a lymph node shows accumulation of antigen-laden fluid in the subcapsular sinus. Which lecture-described cell type in the subcapsular sinus initially encounters this antigen?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus macrophages capture antigen from afferent lymph
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates subcapsular sinus contains macrophages that filter afferent lymph and capture antigen.

options.text: Follicular B cells in cortex immediately phagocytose incoming antigen
options.rationale: Wrong because B cells recognize antigen via receptors but macrophages in sinuses first capture particulate antigen; if antigen were soluble and reached follicles, B cells could bind it.

options.text: High endothelial venule endothelial cells present antigen to lymphocytes
options.rationale: Wrong because HEVs mediate lymphocyte entry from blood, not primary antigen capture from afferent lymph; if HEVs expressed antigen presentation molecules, they might present antigen.

options.text: Medullary cord plasma cells neutralize antigen within subcapsular sinus
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells secrete antibody in medullary cords and are not the first-line antigen capturers in subcapsular sinuses; if antibodies were preexisting, they could neutralize antigen.

options.text: Trabecular sinus endothelial cells actively phagocytose particulate antigen
options.rationale: Wrong because trabecular sinuses are conduits and endothelial phagocytosis is not the primary mechanism; macrophages perform most phagocytosis in sinuses.

question: A patient with a congenital defect preventing B cell migration to medullary cords would most likely have which functional deficit described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Reduced plasma cell antibody secretion into lymph node sinuses
options.rationale: Correct because medullary cords contain plasma cells that secrete antibodies into medullary sinuses; impaired migration reduces local antibody secretion.

options.text: Loss of T cell activation within paracortex due to absent B cells
options.rationale: Wrong because T cell activation primarily occurs in paracortex and does not require B cell migration to medullary cords; if T cell help were absent, B cell responses would be affected.

options.text: Impaired antigen capture by subcapsular sinus macrophages
options.rationale: Wrong because macrophage antigen capture in subcapsular sinus is independent of B cell migration; if macrophage numbers fell, capture would be impaired.

options.text: Failure of germinal center formation in cortical follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal center formation occurs in follicles and may still occur even if migration to medulla is impaired; if B cells could not proliferate, germinal centers would be absent.

options.text: Absence of high endothelial venules preventing lymphocyte entry
options.rationale: Wrong because HEVs are structural and unrelated to B cell migration to medullary cords; if HEVs were absent, lymphocyte entry would be broadly impaired.

question: A patient with a splenic central arteriole lesion shows disrupted PALS architecture. Which immune cell population in PALS would be most directly affected according to the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: T lymphocytes concentrated in periarteriolar lymphoid sheath
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture identifies PALS as mostly T cells surrounding central arterioles; damage disrupts T cell localization and responses to bloodborne antigens.

options.text: B lymphocytes forming germinal centers in marginal zone follicles
options.rationale: Wrong because germinal centers are in white pulp follicles, not PALS; if follicles adjacent to PALS were damaged, B cell responses would be affected.

options.text: Plasma cells in red pulp secreting antibodies into circulation
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells are mainly in medullary cords of nodes and red pulp, not PALS; if plasma cell niches were destroyed, antibody secretion would fall.

options.text: Marginal zone macrophages capturing antigens from lymphatics
options.rationale: Wrong because marginal zone macrophages capture blood antigens between red and white pulp, not lymphatics; if marginal zone were injured, antigen capture would be impaired.

options.text: Endothelial cells of splenic vein regulating venous outflow
options.rationale: Wrong because endothelial cells regulate vascular flow but PALS disruption specifically affects T cells; if venous endothelium were damaged, outflow issues would arise.

question: A histology slide shows Hassall corpuscles in the medulla and abundant epithelial cell staining. Which organ and function described in the lecture does this indicate?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Thymic medulla with epithelial cells aiding regulatory T cell development
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture notes Hassall corpuscles are epithelial cell groups in thymic medulla important for regulatory T cell formation.

options.text: Lymph node medulla with epithelial cells forming Hassall corpuscles
options.rationale: Wrong because Hassall corpuscles are unique to thymus; if the node were ectopically thymic tissue, this would be unusual but possible.

options.text: Spleen white pulp with epithelial Hassall corpuscle structures
options.rationale: Wrong because spleen lacks Hassall corpuscles; if epithelial inclusions were present in spleen, they would represent different pathology.

options.text: Palatine tonsil crypt epithelium forming Hassall corpuscles
options.rationale: Wrong because tonsillar crypts are epithelial invaginations but do not form Hassall corpuscles; if the tissue were thymic, crypts would not be expected.

options.text: Bone marrow epithelial islands resembling Hassall corpuscles
options.rationale: Wrong because bone marrow does not contain Hassall corpuscles; if marrow stromal cells formed epithelial-like clusters, they would be distinct from thymic Hassall corpuscles.

question: A patient with impaired lymph formation due to decreased interstitial fluid return would most likely show which clinical sign described in the lecture?
cited_learning_objective: Formation of Lymph

options.text: Peripheral edema from accumulation of interstitial fluid and lymph
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture explains that lymph returns interstitial fluid to circulation and impaired return leads to edema.

options.text: Pallor from reduced erythrocyte recycling in red pulp
options.rationale: Wrong because pallor from reduced erythrocyte recycling relates to splenic red pulp dysfunction, not primary lymph formation; if spleen function were lost, anemia risk could increase.

options.text: Lymphadenopathy due to increased HEV mediated lymphocyte entry
options.rationale: Wrong because lymphadenopathy from increased HEV activity is not the direct result of decreased lymph return; if HEVs were hyperactive, node enlargement might occur.

options.text: Hypogammaglobulinemia from failure of germinal center formation
options.rationale: Wrong because hypogammaglobulinemia stems from impaired B cell responses, not directly from lymph formation defects; if germinal centers failed, antibody levels would fall.

options.text: Splenomegaly from congestion of red pulp and venous stasis
options.rationale: Wrong because splenomegaly is related to splenic blood flow and congestion, not primary lymph formation; if venous outflow were obstructed, splenomegaly could result.

question: A patient with recurrent respiratory infections has hypertrophy of bronchus-associated lymphoid tissue. According to the lecture, what is the primary role of BALT in such infections?
cited_learning_objective: List, verbally describe, and visually identify the organs specific to the immune system (thymus, lymph node, mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, & spleen) and their structural and cellular components.

options.text: BALT functions like lymph nodes to protect mucosal surfaces from pathogens
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture states MALT components including BALT function similarly to lymph nodes to protect mucosal surfaces from infective organisms.

options.text: BALT primarily filters aged erythrocytes from pulmonary circulation
options.rationale: Wrong because erythrocyte filtration is a splenic red pulp function, not BALT; if pulmonary circulation had splenic-like tissue, this would be atypical.

options.text: BALT is the main site of T cell maturation and selection
options.rationale: Wrong because T cell maturation occurs in thymus; BALT supports peripheral immune responses at mucosa rather than central maturation.

options.text: BALT secretes systemic IgG from resident plasma cells into blood
options.rationale: Wrong because BALT supports mucosal immunity and IgA production more than systemic IgG; if plasma cells produced IgG locally, some systemic spillover could occur.

options.text: BALT forms Hassall corpuscles to generate regulatory T cells
options.rationale: Wrong because Hassall corpuscles are thymic medullary epithelial structures; BALT does not form Hassall corpuscles.

question: A patient with a lymph node excision shows absence of follicles but preserved paracortex. Which immune deficit described in the lecture would you expect?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Impaired B cell clonal expansion and memory B cell production
options.rationale: Correct because cortical follicles are the site of B cell activation and germinal center formation; their absence impairs B cell clonal expansion and memory formation.

options.text: Loss of naive T cell entry due to absent high endothelial venules
options.rationale: Wrong because paracortex preservation suggests HEVs may be intact; if HEVs were absent, naive T cell entry would be reduced.

options.text: Failure of plasma cell antibody secretion in medullary cords
options.rationale: Wrong because plasma cells derive from activated B cells in follicles; if follicles are absent, plasma cell numbers may fall but medullary cords could still contain residual plasma cells.

options.text: Inability to filter afferent lymph in subcapsular sinus macrophages
options.rationale: Wrong because subcapsular sinus macrophages filter lymph independently of follicles; if macrophages were depleted, antigen capture would be impaired.

options.text: Defect in thymic T cell education leading to autoimmunity
options.rationale: Wrong because thymic education occurs in thymus, not lymph node follicles; if thymic function were abnormal, central tolerance would be affected.

question: A patient with a localized skin infection shows migration of dendritic cells carrying antigen to the nearest lymph node. According to the lecture, which lymph node zone will these dendritic cells most likely enter to present antigen to T cells?
cited_learning_objective: Describe the functional areas of the lymph tissues and their roles in immune responses.

options.text: Paracortex where antigen presenting cells activate naive T lymphocytes
options.rationale: Correct because the lecture indicates the paracortex is the T cell zone and main site of antigen presentation and T cell activation by dendritic cells.

options.text: Cortical follicles where dendritic cells stimulate B cell proliferation
options.rationale: Wrong because follicles are B cell zones; if dendritic cells presented antigen to follicular B cells, they would localize to follicles but primary T cell activation occurs in paracortex.

options.text: Medullary cords where dendritic cells differentiate into plasma cells
options.rationale: Wrong because medullary cords contain plasma cells and macrophages, not dendritic cell–driven T cell activation; if dendritic cells induced plasmacytic differentiation, this would be atypical.

options.text: Subcapsular sinus where dendritic cells become follicular dendritic cells
options.rationale: Wrong because follicular dendritic cells are stromal cells in follicles, not derived from migrating dendritic cells; if antigen were retained in sinus, macrophages would capture it.

options.text: Hilum where dendritic cells exit node into efferent lymphatics
options.rationale: Wrong because dendritic cells enter nodes via afferent lymphatics and present antigen in paracortex; if dendritic cells failed to present antigen, they might exit via efferent vessels.
